statement: Multiple Icelandic governments have promised or discussed holding a referendum on EU accession without delivering one, creating a persistent source of political frustration. Key instances: (1) The 2009–2013 coalition of Samfylkingin and VG submitted Iceland's EU application in July 2009 with the stated intention of holding a referendum on any accession treaty, but the application was suspended before reaching that stage. (2) The 2013–2016 coalition (Progressive Party and Independence Party) pledged in its coalition agreement to withdraw the EU application, which was formally done in March 2015 — Foreign Minister Gunnar Bragi Sveinsson stated that no referendum was needed because the application was being withdrawn, not advanced. (3) The 2021–2024 coalition did not include an EU referendum in its programme. The 2024 coalition (Samfylkingin, Viðreisn, Flokkur fólksins) is the first government to explicitly commit to holding a referendum on resuming accession negotiations, scheduling it for 29 August 2026.
source_name: Althingi records; coalition agreements 2009, 2013, 2017, 2021, 2024; Icelandic media archives
caveats: Whether previous governments 'promised' a referendum depends on interpretation. The 2009 coalition committed to a referendum on any final accession treaty (which was never reached), not on whether to start negotiations. The characterisation that governments 'didn't keep their promise' conflates different types of commitments. Only the 2024 coalition has made an explicit commitment to a referendum on resuming negotiations per se.

statement: Samtök iðnaðarins (SI, Confederation of Icelandic Enterprise) conducted a membership survey in February/March 2026 on attitudes toward Iceland's potential EU membership. The survey found that 57% of SI members were opposed to EU membership (þar af 43% mjög andvígir — of which 43% strongly opposed), while a minority supported membership. The proportion of members reporting strong opposition had approximately doubled compared to a comparable survey conducted in 2024. SI is Iceland's largest employers' organisation, representing companies across manufacturing, technology, services, and other sectors. The organisation has historically taken a cautious position on EU membership, emphasising both the potential benefits of single market access and the risks of EU regulation, particularly for Iceland's specific economic conditions. SI's membership includes both exporters (who might benefit from closer EU integration) and domestically oriented businesses (who might face increased competition).
caveats: Employer organisation surveys reflect the views of business owners and managers, not the general public or workers. The doubling of strong opposition may reflect sampling variation, changes in SI membership composition, or genuine shifts in business sentiment. The survey methodology (sample size, response rate, question wording) has not been independently verified. SI's public position on EU membership is more nuanced than the survey results might suggest — the organisation has called for thorough economic analysis before any decision.

statement: Bændasamtök Íslands (Icelandic Farmers' Association, BÍ) has consistently expressed serious concerns about the impact of EU membership on Icelandic agriculture. BÍ's stated position is that while it does not oppose a referendum per se, it considers EU membership itself to pose existential risks to Icelandic farming. Key concerns include: the removal of tariff protection under the EU customs union would expose Icelandic farmers to competition from larger, lower-cost EU producers; the transition from Iceland's national support system to CAP may not adequately compensate for Iceland's high production costs (sub-Arctic climate, small scale, geographic isolation); food safety and animal health standards could be compromised (Iceland has been largely free of diseases common in continental European livestock). BÍ has also called attention to what it describes as 'upplýsingaóreiða' (information disorder/misinformation) in the EU debate, arguing that some pro-EU voices understate the challenges for agriculture.
source_name: Bændasamtök Íslands — Official statements and press releases
caveats: BÍ is an advocacy organisation representing farmers' interests and its position reflects those interests. Some economists and pro-EU commentators argue that BÍ overstates the risks and understates the potential benefits of CAP participation (direct payments, rural development funding, access to EU food markets). The impact on Icelandic agriculture would depend heavily on transition arrangements negotiated during accession — Finland and Sweden's experience suggests that Arctic/sub-Arctic supplements are possible.

statement: Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir, leader of Viðreisn (Reform Party), serves as utanríkisráðherra (Foreign Minister) in the coalition government formed in late 2024. She has been Viðreisn's leader since 2018 and an Althingi MP since 2016. As Foreign Minister, she oversees the EU referendum process and has been the government's primary spokesperson on the question. She announced the referendum date of 29 August 2026.
source_name: Althingi — Member directory; Government of Iceland cabinet listing
caveats: The combination of party leadership and the relevant ministerial portfolio is common in Icelandic coalition politics — it is standard practice for senior coalition party leaders to hold key ministerial positions aligned with their party's policy priorities.
statement_is: Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir, formaður Viðreisnar og utanríkisráðherra, hefur verið aðaltalsmaður ríkisstjórnarinnar um þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsluna og leiðir ESB-ferlið. Hún tilkynnti dagsetninguna 29. ágúst 2026.
caveats_is: Það er algengt í íslenskum samsteypustjórnum að formaður stjórnmálaflokks fari jafnframt með það ráðherraembætti sem mestu máli skiptir fyrir flokkinn – það er hefð fyrir því að helstu leiðtogar stjórnarflokkanna gegni lykilráðherraembættum í samræmi við stefnumál flokka sinna.

statement: Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party), founded in 2017 by Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson, is affiliated with the European Conservatives and Reformists (ECR) group at the European level, which includes parties such as the Italian Fratelli d'Italia (led by PM Giorgia Meloni), the Polish Law and Justice party (PiS), and the Sweden Democrats. The party has positioned itself as Eurosceptic and sovereigntist. Reports in Icelandic media have noted contacts between Miðflokkurinn representatives and US Republican Party circles, though the nature and extent of these contacts are not fully documented in public records. Sigmundur Davíð has characterised the government's EU referendum as 'Evrópubrölt' (roughly: 'EU agitation/hustle') and accused the government of concealing its pro-EU agenda from the public and from Althingi.
source_name: Miðflokkurinn — Party website and statements; ECR Party membership; Icelandic media reports
caveats: Political party connections across borders are normal in democratic politics — most Icelandic parties have international affiliations. The characterisation of contacts with the US Republican Party comes primarily from political opponents and media reports, and the significance of any such contacts is disputed. Sigmundur Davíð's 'Evrópubrölt' characterisation is a political opinion, not a factual claim.
